slides.title: Flask Fundamentals
slides.subtitle:
The Minimalist Web Framework for Python
Scale, Extensions, and Production Architecture

slides.name: Aravind Govindhasamy
slides.title: Software Developer
slides.experience: Specialist in Python, .NET, and Scalable Backend Systems
slides.oss_experience: Open Source Contributor & Technical Content Creator

box.content: Minimalist by design. Powerful by choice.
Flask is a micro-framework based on Werkzeug and Jinja2. It doesn't make decisions for you (like database choice or validation).

list.text: Micro-framework: Core features are kept simple but extensible.
Total Control: You choose every part of your stack (SQLAlchemy, Mongo, etc.).
Scalable Architecture: Start small, grow into a modular system.

box.content: Unlike Django, Flask is unopinionated. It provides a solid foundation without enforcing a specific project structure.

list.text: Flexibility: Ideal for microservices and specialized APIs.
Extension Ecosystem: Add only what you need (Auth, Admin, Migrations).
Modern Python: Leveraging decorators and latest language features.

box.content: The logic of URL dispatch.
Binding functions to URLs is the heart of Flask. Simple, readable, and powerful.
Flask allows you to specify variable types directly in the URL rule.

list.text: int: Accepts integer values.
float: Accepts floating point values.
path: Like string but also accepts slashes.
uuid: Accepts UUID strings.

box.content: Explicitly define which HTTP methods a route should handle.
Handling incoming data.
The global-like 'request' object provides access to everything from form data to headers.

list.text: request.form: For POST/PUT form data.
request.args: For URL parameters (?key=value).
request.files: For uploaded files.
request.cookies: To read cookies.

box.content: Flask uses cryptographically signed cookies for sessions. This allows you to store data across requests without server-side state by default.
HTML generated by Python.
Jinja2 provides a safe and expressive way to render HTML dynamically.

list.text: {{ value }}: Expressions to print to the template output.
{% if condition %}: Logic statements (loops, conditionals).
{# comments #}: Template comments.

box.content: Use 'blocks' and 'extends' to keep your HTML structure DRY.
How to structure Flask for growth.
Avoid the 'circular import' trap by creating your app instance inside a function.
box.code:
def create_app():
    app = Flask(__name__)
    app.config.from_object('config.Config')
    return app

box.content: Blueprints allow you to organize your application into distinct components, making it easier to scale and reuse code.
Class-based views for Flask that allow for better code reuse and inheritance patterns.
The 'Batteries' of the Flask world.
The most popular way to add an ORM to Flask. It simplifies database interactions significantly.
box.code:
db = SQLAlchemy(app)

class User(db.Model):
    id = db.Column(db.Integer, primary_key=True)
    username = db.Column(db.String(80), unique=True)

box.content: Handle database migrations using Alembic, integrated directly into the Flask CLI.
box.code:
migrate = Migrate(app, db)
# shell: flask db migrate -m "Initial"

box.content: Easily handle user sessions and Cross-Origin Resource Sharing for your APIs.

list.text: Flask-Login: Manages authentication state.
Flask-CORS: Simple setup for cross-origin requests.

box.content: Understanding the Context Locals.
Flask uses contexts to make certain objects globally accessible during a request without passing them around.

list.text: Request Context: Keeps track of request-level data (request, session).
Application Context: Keeps track of app-level data (current_app, g).

box.content: The 'g' object is a namespace for storing data during a single request. It's unique to each request.
Flask uses werkzeug.local to ensure that 'current_app' and 'request' always point to the correct instance even in multi-threaded environments.
box.code:
from flask import current_app
# Works even if multiple app instances exist

box.content: Objects like 'request' are actually proxies. Calling them redirects to the real object sitting in the current context.
Signals, CLI, and Modern Async.
Flask supports signals via the Blinker library. Decouple your app logic by subscribing to events.
Add your own commands to the 'flask' executable using the @app.cli.command() decorator.
Modern Flask supports async def for view functions, allowing you to use await for I/O bound tasks.
Centralize your error logic using the @app.errorhandler decorator.
Building robust RESTful services.
Use jsonify() to return JSON data with the correct content-type header.
Access JSON payloads from the client using request.get_json().
For complex schemas and resources, extensions provide structured validation and serialization.

list.text: Flask-RESTful: Adds support for quickly building REST APIs.
Marshmallow: Object serialization and validation library.

box.content: Use Flasgger or similar tools to automatically generate Swagger/OpenAPI documentation.
box.code:
from flasgger import Swagger
Swagger(app)

box.content: Scaling Flask for the Real World.
Never use the built-in development server in production. Use a robust WSGI HTTP Server.

list.text: Gunicorn: A Python WSGI HTTP Server for UNIX.
uWSGI: A full stack for building hosting services.

box.content: Use Nginx as a reverse proxy to handle SSL termination, buffering, and static file serving.
Protect your app with essential security headers like HSTS and X-Content-Type-Options.
box.code:
from flask_talisman import Talisman
Talisman(app)

box.content: Configure Python's logging module to capture errors and performance metrics in production.
box.code: app.logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)

box.content: For high-traffic apps, consider horizontal scaling and task queues.

list.text: Load Balancing: Distribute traffic across multiple instances.
Task Queues: Offload heavy work to Celery or RQ.

box.content: Standard tools for reliability.
Pytest is the standard for Python testing. Use fixtures to manage your app and database state.
Test code that depends on the request context using test_request_context().
box.code:
with app.test_request_context('/'):
    assert request.path == '/'

box.content: Test the full request flow, including database interactions and external API calls.

slides.title: Questions? 💬
slides.content:
Let's discuss Flask architecture, the extension ecosystem, or production best practices!
Happy to answer anything Flask-related.

box.content:
🏺 Go build something amazing with Flask.
Happy Coding!
